items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: What is the difference between an IP address and a MAC address?
items.correct: MAC is a hardware identifier (fixed); IP is a logical network address (assignable).
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: IP is for input, MAC is for output.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. MAC is burned into the network card (Layer 2); IP is assigned by the network (Layer 3). Both are needed to route packets to a device.

items.q: Why is UDP used for live video streaming despite being unreliable?
items.correct: Lower overhead and latency - retransmitting late packets would harm real-time quality.
items.misconception: Because TCP cannot handle video data.
items.random_wrong: Because UDP is always faster than TCP.
items.why: Students assume reliability is always desirable. For real-time media, a dropped packet is preferable to a delayed one - UDP trades reliability for timeliness, which suits live media.
The answer examiners want is: Gradients of loss. “Forward outputs” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which problem cannot be solved by a single perceptron?
items.why: Correct answer: XOR. “AND” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many layers does a perceptron model have?
items.why: The real figure is 1. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
